step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("recent studies on self-efficacy mediation between transformational leadership and sleep quality in healthcare workers")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

After reviewing the previous discussion and retrieving recent studies on self-efficacy mediation between transformational leadership and sleep quality in healthcare workers, here is my revised analysis:
I find that there is limited direct evidence linking self-efficacy as a mediator between transformational leadership and sleep quality in healthcare workers. Most of the retrieved materials emphasize indirect pathways such as improved team collaboration, enhanced conflict management, better communication, and reduction in fatigue due to optimized scheduling—all of which may theoretically influence sleep quality. However, none of the sources explicitly mention self-efficacy being a key intermediate variable facilitating this link. Therefore, although transformational leadership might positively affect sleep through fostering trust and teamwork, the proposed mediating role of self-efficacy remains unsubstantiated by current data.
Answer choice: \boxed{B}
Even though transformational leadership promotes positive work cultures and team cohesion—which can indirectly benefit sleep—a clear causal pathway mediated by self-efficacy is absent in the retrieved literature. Without explicit mechanistic evidence showing how perceived capability (self-efficacy) translates into improvements in sleep quality following leadership intervention, we cannot reasonably conclude that self-efficacy acts as a mediator.
\boxed{B} Based on updated evidence, I continue to disagree with option A. While transformational leadership contributes to healthier work environments and reduced fatigue through strong team dynamics, no direct or indirect evidence establishes self-efficacy as a mediating factor between these leadership behaviors and improved sleep quality among healthcare workers. Thus, the claim lacks empirical grounding in current research.
